perturbation.original_text: 1.1 In this Agreement, “Confidential Informati on” means all information, including, but not limited to; • Facility layout, office / room locations, and contents, • Facility design and construction materials used, • Physical facility security features , alarms and access control systems, • Any diagrams or drawings related to the facility or any of its systems, • Number of facility empl oyees and staffing levels, • Personal information about Reve nue Operations employees, • Information related to employee work schedules and driver routes, • Revenue processing equipment and processing operations, • Type and/or quantity of financial assets on-hand, • Type and/or quantity of financial asse ts collected or processed daily, • Time, frequency and amount of bank deposits, • General revenue information and statistical data, • Numbers and locations of surveillance cameras, • Information about Revenue Operations fl eet vehicles & security features, • Information about ATA client accounts, • Information about to Revenue Oper ations equipment & prototypes, • Any special security featur es of TTC fare media types, • Operating policies and procedures pertaini ng to the day-to-day activities of the Revenue Operati ons Department, whether disclosed in writing, orally , machine readable form, through personal observation(s), or third party word of mouth.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 In this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means only the specific documents marked as confidential in writing by the TTC. It does not include orally disclosed information, information learned through observation, or general knowledge about TTC operations, regardless of how it was obtained.  Specifically excluded is information learned through observation or general knowledge of the TTC operations.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction. The original definition of 'Confidential Information' is broad and includes various types of information, disclosed in multiple ways, including observations. The modified definition narrows it significantly, contradicting the original expansive scope by excluding observed or orally disclosed information. This contradicts the broader list originally set and creates uncertainty about what is considered confidential, which can be found throughout the document, but primarily in Clause 2.
perturbation.contradicted_location: CLAUSE 2. USE AND DISCLOSURE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.1.1
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 The Company shall: 2.1.1 Use the Confidential Information sole ly for the Purpose as set out above;
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' broadly, including information disclosed orally or through observation. The changed text significantly narrows this definition to only specific documents marked as confidential in writing, explicitly excluding orally disclosed information and information learned through observation. Clause 2.1.1 states that the company shall use the Confidential Information solely for the Purpose as set out above. Because the definition of 'Confidential Information' is drastically changed, this creates a contradiction between what constitutes confidential information. Therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1.4 Restrict the release, access and us e of the Confidenti al Information to those employees and officers who must have access to the Confidential Information in order to pe rmit the Company, in good fa ith, to determine whether to submit a bid based on the RFP, sa ve and except as otherwise provided herein; 2.1.5 Ensure that each person to whom C onfidential Information is disclosed to in accordance with Clause 2.1.4 is advis ed, prior to the di sclosure, of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1.4 The company may release and use the information as it sees fit, in the company's best interest. 2.1.5 Confidentiality obligations do not extend to any individual outside of the direct employment of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The original text strictly limits access to Confidential Information and mandates advising recipients of its confidential nature. The modified clause completely undermines this by allowing unrestricted release and eliminating confidentiality obligations for those outside direct employment. This contradicts the entire premise of Clause 2 and introduces an in-text contradiction by voiding the intended confidentiality of the information. This contradicts specifically 2.1.1 since the release is no longer solely for the stated purpose.
perturbation.location: CLAUSE 2. USE AND DISCLOSURE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.1.4
perturbation.contradicted_location: CLAUSE 2. USE AND DISCLOSURE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.1.1
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 The Company shall: 2.1.1 Use the Confidential Information sole ly for the Purpose as set out above;
perturbation.justification: The changed text in 2.1.4 and 2.1.5 directly contradicts 2.1.1. The original 2.1.4 restricts the release and use of Confidential Information, while the changed version allows the company to release and use the information as it sees fit, regardless of the purpose outlined in 2.1.1. This unrestricted release directly conflicts with 2.1.1, which mandates that Confidential Information be used solely for a specific purpose. This creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 Immediately upon written request by the TTC and subject to any statutory requirement, the Company shall return all copies of the Confid ential Information in its possession to the requesting party or certify that all copies in its possession or control have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 The Company is under no obligation to return or destroy any Confidential Information, regardless of any request from the TTC.
perturbation.explanation: This clause directly contradicts the original requirement for the Company to return or destroy Confidential Information upon request. By stating the Company has no such obligation, it undermines TTC's control over its confidential information and creates uncertainty about the enforcement of confidentiality. This creates an in-text contradiction. This specifically contradicts section 2.1.2 since the company is not holding the information in confidence anymore, since it can deny the TTC of requesting the Confidential Information to be returned.
perturbation.location: CLAUSE 2. USE AND DISCLOSURE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.3
perturbation.contradicted_location: CLAUSE 2. USE AND DISCLOSURE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.1.2
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1.2 Hold the Conf idential Information in confiden ce and shall not sell, assign, transfer or otherwise disclose the Confiden tial Information, or any information or materials derived there from, to any thir d party without the prior consent of the TTC, save and except as otherwise provided herein;
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 2.3 explicitly states that "The Company is under no obligation to return or destroy any Confidential Information, regardless of any request from the TTC." This directly contradicts section 2.1.2, which requires the Company to "Hold the Conf idential Information in confiden ce and shall not sell, assign, transfer or otherwise disclose the Confiden tial Information...without the prior consent of the TTC." By removing the obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon request, the Company no longer has to 'hold the Confidential Information in confidence' as requested by the TTC, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Notwithstanding anything to the contra ry herein, the Company’s confidentiality obligations shall not apply to Confidential Information that: 3.1.1 Is known to the Company at the time of disclosure; 3.1.2 Is independently developed by the Co mpany provided that party can show that such development was accomp lished without the use of or any reference to the Confidential Information; 3.1.3 Becomes known to the Com pany from another source without confidentiality restriction on subsequent disclosure or use, provided that such source is not in breach of its obligations of non-disclosure; 3.1.4 Is or becomes a par t of the public domain thr ough no wrongful act of the Company; or 3.1.5 Is subsequently publicly disclosed by the TTC.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 The Company's confidentiality obligations apply to all Confidential Information without exception. The exceptions listed in 3.1.1 through 3.1.5 are null and void.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes all exceptions to confidentiality, directly contradicting the clauses that define those exceptions. By stating that 'all' Confidential Information is subject to confidentiality, it eliminates the scenarios where the Company would be free from those obligations, and creates an in-text contradiction with sections 3.1.1 through 3.1.5. This creates uncertainty about the scope and applicability of the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Notwithstanding anything to the contra ry herein, the Company’s confidentiality obligations shall not apply to Confidential Information that: 3.1.1 Is known to the Company at the time of disclosure;
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the Company's confidentiality obligations apply to 'all' Confidential Information without exception, nullifying the exceptions previously defined in clauses 3.1.1 through 3.1.5. The contradicted text, specifically 3.1.1, states that the confidentiality obligations do *not* apply to information known to the Company at the time of disclosure. This creates a direct and strong contradiction, because the changed text says there are no exceptions and the contradicted text states that there is an exception (information known at the time of disclosure), making the two incompatible.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1.9 Both parties’ obligatio ns of confidentiality sha ll continue for a period of three (3) years following the last discl osure of Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1.9 The Company's confidentiality obligations shall terminate immediately upon submission of a bid based on the RFP, regardless of whether the TTC accepts the bid.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies a three-year confidentiality period. The change establishes that confidentiality terminates upon bid submission. This creates an in-text contradiction, since regardless of the state of the bid, the confidentiality is broken. This creates uncertainty over the confidentiality obligations by introducing two separate conflicting periods. This also may give away details of the RFP to any parties involved and is not limited to only TTC.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In this Agreement, “Confidential Informati on” means all information, including, but not limited to; • Facility layout, office / room locations, and contents, • Facility design and construction materials used, • Physical facility security features , alarms and access control systems, • Any diagrams or drawings related to the facility or any of its systems, • Number of facility empl oyees and staffing levels, • Personal information about Reve nue Operations employees, • Information related to employee work schedules and driver routes, • Revenue processing equipment and processing operations, • Type and/or quantity of financial assets on-hand, • Type and/or quantity of financial asse ts collected or processed daily, • Time, frequency and amount of bank deposits, • General revenue information and statistical data, • Numbers and locations of surveillance cameras, • Information about Revenue Operations fl eet vehicles & security features, • Information about ATA client accounts, • Information about to Revenue Oper ations equipment & prototypes, • Any special security featur es of TTC fare media types, • Operating policies and procedures pertaini ng to the day-to-day activities of the Revenue Operati ons Department, whether disclosed in writing, orally , machine readable form, through personal observation(s), or third party word of mouth.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates a 3-year confidentiality period post-disclosure. The changed text states that confidentiality ends upon bid submission. The 'Contradicted Text' defines 'Confidential Information' to include numerous sensitive details. The changed text directly contradicts the original clause by establishing an immediate termination of confidentiality upon bid submission, regardless of the terms outlined in the definition of Confidential Information. This directly conflicts with the intended protection of Confidential Information as it allows for immediate disclosure of sensitive information, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1.8 This Agreement may only be m odified by written agreement of both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1.8 The TTC reserves the right to unilaterally modify this Agreement at any time without prior notice or consent from the Company. Any such modifications will be effective immediately.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires mutual written agreement for any modification to the agreement. The changed clause permits the TTC to unilaterally modify the agreement at any time, which creates an in-text contradiction since only one party can modify an agreement. This creates uncertainty about how and when the Agreement can be modified, who can modify it, and whether the Agreement can be held legally binding.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.1.5 This Agreement constitutes the co mplete and exclusive statement of the terms and conditions between them with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior and contemporaneous oral or written statements which are in consistent herewith.
perturbation.justification: The original clause 6.1.8 states that the agreement can only be modified by written agreement of both parties. The changed clause 6.1.8 allows the TTC to unilaterally modify the agreement. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, 6.1.5, which states that the agreement is the complete and exclusive statement of terms, implying that changes require mutual agreement and supersedes prior agreements; therefore, one party modifying the contract would render the agreement incomplete and inconsistent with this statement. The ability for only one party to change the entire agreement introduces a clear contradiction, impacting the contract's enforceability and certainty.
